content:
Spotify launched their codes in ~2019 and now there are many services to leverage this feature, enabling you to get a sticker of your favorite songs code among other products like key fobs, mugs, shirts.
The code can be scanned by the Spotify App and will play the scanned song / album / artist.
I’ve seen posts on social media where people would print an albums cover with the corresponding code and hang it onto their walls. I loved the idea and started with a first batch until I discovered that you couldn’t simply drag-and-drop the album cover plus code into your file explorer to get the image.
It wouldn’t appear anywhere in the desktop app and I’ve searched for a long time until I decided to make this a friday night project.
Album Cover For getting the album cover I used SpotiPy:
# SPOTIFY_CLIENT_ID and SPOTIFY_CLIENT_SECRET need to be exported first spotipy = spotipy.Spotify(client_credentials_manager=SpotifyClientCredentials()) results = spotipy.album(album_uri) link_to_cover = results["images"][0]["url"] cover_size = results["images"][0]["height"] Everything I needed was in place; the results were already sorted from highest to lowest dimensions, URL and dimensions are accessible.
Spotify Code Spotify has a website up to download codes only, providing options for background colors, bar color, output format and size.
I discovered that the download link was parameterized (bottom line), which saved me a lot of time.
The covers I’ve seen always matched the mean color of the album art. For this I used colorthief. Size is determined by the album cover size. I plug those values into the URL and use Pillow to download the picture.
Getting mean color from file object Revisiting this the hardest part was trying to mitigate downloading the pictures to temp. I’ve found this snippet on StackOverflow where somebody already came up with the following solution, which I adapted:
with io.BytesIO() as file_object: pillow_image.save(file_object, "PNG") cf = ColorThief(file_object) colours = cf.get_color() Putting it all together Pillow also makes merging two pictures a breeze:
final_height = album_code.size[1] + cover_size im = Image.new(mode="RGB", size=(cover_size, final_height)) im.paste(album_art, (0, 0)) im.paste(album_code, (0, cover_size)) After doing this I’ve added argparse for more robust CLI-behaviour:
/bin/python bbsac/bbsac.py -h usage: bbsac.py [-h] -u URIS [URIS ...] [-o OUT] options: -h, --help show this help message and exit -u URIS [URIS ...] Spotify album URI(s) -o OUT path to output folder Spotify URIs (Uniform Resource Indicator) can be easily obtained through the share button.
While this part is still manual, I’m satisfied with the end result.
My wall now looks like this: You find the repo here.
summary:
Spotify launched their codes in ~2019 and now there are many services to leverage this feature, enabling you to get a sticker of your favorite songs code among other products like key fobs, mugs, shirts.
The code can be scanned by the Spotify App and will play the scanned song / album / artist.
I’ve seen posts on social media where people would print an albums cover with the corresponding code and hang it onto their walls.
title: Bring Back Spotify Album Codes!
